title: Self-suspending proppants for use in carbon dioxide-based fracturing fluids and methods of making and use thereof
abstract: Self-suspending proppants including proppant particles coated with a CO2-philic coating are provided. The CO2-philic coating may be lightly crosslinked and may have a physical structure that constrains CO2 molecules. Methods of making self-suspending proppants may include coating a proppant particle with a polymerizable precursor material of a CO2-philic material and polymerizing the polymerizable precursor material to form a self-suspending proppant are also provided. Additionally, hydraulic fracturing fluids that may include a CO2 based fluid and the self-suspending proppants and methods of treating subterranean formations by contacting a subterranean formation with hydraulic fracturing fluid and propagating at least one subterranean fracture are provided.
claims: Claims 1. A self-suspending proppant comprising proppant particles coated with a C0 2 -philic coating, in which the C0 2 -philic coating is lightly crosslinked and has a physical structure that constrains C0 2 molecules. 2. The self-suspending proppant of claim 1, in which the self-suspending proppant volumetrically expands from a non- swollen state to a swollen state when C0 2 molecules are constrained within the physical structure. 3. The self-suspending proppant of claim 2, in which the physical structure of the C0 2 -philic coating volumetrically expands by at least 20% from the non-swollen state to the swollen state. 4. The self-suspending proppant of claims 2 or 3, in which the physical structure of the C0 2 -philic coating volumetrically expands by at least 100% from the non-swollen state to the swollen state. 5. The self-suspending proppant of any of claims 2 to 4, in which the physical structure of the C0 2 -philic coating volumetrically expands by at least 300% from the non-swollen state to the swollen state. 6. The self-suspending proppant of any of the preceding claims, in which the proppant particles have a rough surface with an arithmetic average roughness (R a ) of greater than or equal to 1 nanometer (nm). 7. The self-suspending proppant of any of the preceding claims, in which the C0 2 -philic coating comprises a C0 2 -philic material chosen from polysaccharide acetates, polyethylene glycols, partially fluorinated oxygen-containing polymers, oxygenated polymers, crosslinked oxygen-containing polystyrenes, polyvinyl acetates, and combinations of any of these. 8. The self-suspending proppant of any of the preceding claims, in which the proppant particles are particles of a material selected from the group consisting of oxides, silicates, sand, ceramic, resin, plastic, mineral, glass, graded sand, treated sand, resin-coated sand, other resin- coated particles, bauxite, sintered bauxite, glass particles, glass beads, and combinations thereof. 9. The self-suspending proppant of any of the preceding claims, in which the C0 2 -philic coating does not dissociate from the proppant particles when the self-suspending proppant is added to a water-based fluid. 10. A hydraulic fracturing fluid comprising: a fluid comprising C0 2 , and self-suspending proppants suspended in the fluid, the self-suspending proppants comprising proppant particles coated with a C0 2 -philic coating in which the C0 2 - philic coating is lightly crosslinked and has a physical structure that constrains C0 2 molecules. 11. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of claim 10, in which the fluid comprises liquid C0 2 , supercritical C0 2 , critical C0 2 , subcritical C0 2 , or combinations thereof. 12. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any one of claims 10 or 11, in which the fluid further comprises N 2 . 13. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 10 to 12, in which the physical structure of the C0 2 -philic coating comprises constrained C0 2 molecules that volumetrically expand the self-suspending proppants to a swollen state. 14. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of claim 13, in which the physical structure of the C0 2 - philic coating is volumetrically expanded by at least 20% from a non-swollen state to the swollen state. 15. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of claim 13 or 14, in which the physical structure of the C0 2 -philic coating is volumetrically expanded by at least 100% from the non- swollen state to the swollen state. 16. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 13 to 15, in which the physical structure of the C0 2 -philic coating is volumetrically expanded by at least 300% from the non-swollen state to the swollen state. 17. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 10 to 16, in which the self-suspending proppants in the swollen state have a density of from 25% to 200% of the density of the fluid. 18. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 10 to 17, in which the self-suspending proppants in the swollen state have a density of less than or equal to 200% of the density of the fluid. 19. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 10 to 18, in which the proppant particles have rough surfaces with an arithmetic average roughness (R a ) of greater than or equal to 1 nm. 20. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 10 to 19, in which the C0 2 -philic coating comprises a C0 2 -philic material chosen from polysaccharide acetates, polyethylene glycols, partially fluorinated oxygen-containing polymers, oxygenated polymers, crosslinked oxygen- containing polystyrenes, polyvinyl acetates, and combinations of any of these. 21. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 10 to 20, in which the proppant particles are particles of a material selected from the group consisting of oxides, silicates, sand, ceramic, resin, plastic, mineral, glass, graded sand, treated sand, resin-coated sand, other resin-coated particles, bauxite, sintered bauxite, glass particles, glass beads, and combinations thereof. 22. The hydraulic fracturing fluid of any of claims 10 to 21, in which the C0 2 -philic coating does not dissociate from the proppant particles when the self-suspending proppants are added to a water-based fluid. 23. A method for producing self-suspending proppant particles, the method comprising: coating proppant particles with a polymerizable precursor material of a C0 2 -philic material to form coated proppant particles; and polymerizing the polymerizable precursor material to convert the coated proppant particles to self-suspending proppant particles coated with a C0 2 -philic coating of the C0 2 -philic material, the C0 2 -philic coating being lightly crosslinked and having a physical structure that constrains C0 2 molecules. 24. The method of claim 23, in which coating the proppant particles comprises contacting the polymerizable precursor material with the proppant particles in a fluidized bed process. 25. The method of any one of claims 23 to 24, further comprising coating the proppant particles with an adhesion promoter before coating the proppant particles with the polymerizable precursor material of the C0 2 -philic material. 26. The method of any one of claims 23 to 25, further comprising roughening the proppant particles before the coating step. 27. The method of claim 26, in which roughening the proppant particles produces proppant particles having a rough surface with an arithmetic average roughness (R a ) of greater than or equal to 1 nm. 28. The method of any of claims 23 to 27, in which polymerizing the polymerizable precursor material comprises heating the coated proppant particles at a polymerization temperature and for a polymerization time sufficient to crosslink at least a portion of the polymerizable precursor material. 29. The method of any one of claims 23 to 28, in which polymerizing the polymerizable precursor material comprises exposing the coated proppant particles to ultraviolet (UV) light at conditions sufficient to crosslink at least a portion of the polymerizable precursor material. 30. The method of any of claims 23 to 29, in which the C0 2 -philic material is chosen from polysaccharide acetates, polyethylene glycols, partially fluorinated oxygen-containing polymers, oxygenated polymers, crosslinked oxygen-containing polystyrenes, polyvinyl acetates, and combinations of any of these. 31. The method of any of claims 23 to 30, in which the proppant particles are particles of a material selected from the group consisting of oxides, silicates, sand, ceramic, resin, plastic, mineral, glass, graded sand, treated sand, resin-coated sand, other resin-coated particles, bauxite, sintered bauxite, glass particles, glass beads, and combinations thereof. 32. The method of any of claims 23 to 31, in which the C0 2 -philic coating does not dissociate from the proppant particles when the self-suspending proppant particles are added to a water-based fluid. 33. A method of treating a subterranean formation, the method comprising: contacting a subterranean formation with a hydraulic fracturing fluid according to any one of claims 23 to 32, and propagating at least one subterranean fracture in the subterranean formation with the hydraulic fracturing fluid. 34. The method of claim 33, in which contacting the subterranean formation comprises pumping the hydraulic fracturing fluid into the subterranean formation. 35. The method of claim 33 or 34, in which contacting the subterranean formation comprises: drilling into the subterranean formation; and injecting the hydraulic fracturing fluid into the at least one subterranean fracture in the subterranean formation. 36. The method of any one of claims 33 to 35, in which the subterranean formation comprises a rock formation. 37. The method of any one of claims 33 to 36, in which the subterranean formation comprises shale.
